coredata.dc:title: Pulsed laser fragmentation synthesis of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as fluorescent probes in non-enzymatic glucose detection

coredata.dc:creator: Cortes, Francis Rey U.
Falomir, Eva
Lancis, Jesús
Mínguez-Vega, Gladys
coredata.dc:description: Pulsed laser fragmentation in liquid (PLFL) is one of the synthesis routes for producing high-purity carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with less toxic by-products in a straightforward system. The limited literature regarding the application of PLFL-synthesized CQDs motivated this study to exploit them as fluorescent probes in glucose sensing. The design of a fluorescent-based non-enzymatic glucose sensor was achieved by implementing CQDs and 3-aminophenylboronic acid (APBA) as fluorescent probes and glucose receptors, respectively. The CQDs were fabricated by PLFL through an Nd:YAG nanosecond laser, starting from the carbon powder dispersion in ethanol. Then, a laser-assisted post-modification process with oxidizing acid was implemented to produce water-dispersible CQDs for easy functionalization. Functionalizing these CQDs with APBA (CQDs-APBA) resulted in a blue-shifted fluorescence emission with a 35% quantum yield and high photostability. The CQDs-APBA exhibited a turn-off response at increasing glucose concentrations. This method offers good sensitivity with a linear detection range of glucose with a concentration of 0.165 to 8 mM and a detection limit of 165 µM. The applicability of this sensor to real analytes such as saliva obtained a satisfactory result with good reproducibility. This work proves that CQDs synthesized from PLFL can also be an alternative fluorescent probe for fluorescent-based sensing applications.

head.ce:title: Pulsed laser fragmentation synthesis of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as fluorescent probes in non-enzymatic glucose detection

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Pulsed laser fragmentation in liquid (PLFL) is one of the synthesis routes for producing high-purity carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with less toxic by-products in a straightforward system. The limited literature regarding the application of PLFL-synthesized CQDs motivated this study to exploit them as fluorescent probes in glucose sensing. The design of a fluorescent-based non-enzymatic glucose sensor was achieved by implementing CQDs and 3-aminophenylboronic acid (APBA) as fluorescent probes and glucose receptors, respectively. The CQDs were fabricated by PLFL through an Nd:YAG nanosecond laser, starting from the carbon powder dispersion in ethanol. Then, a laser-assisted post-modification process with oxidizing acid was implemented to produce water-dispersible CQDs for easy functionalization. Functionalizing these CQDs with APBA (CQDs-APBA) resulted in a blue-shifted fluorescence emission with a 35% quantum yield and high photostability. The CQDs-APBA exhibited a turn-off response at increasing glucose concentrations. This method offers good sensitivity with a linear detection range of glucose with a concentration of 0.165 to 8 mM and a detection limit of 165 µM. The applicability of this sensor to real analytes such as saliva obtained a satisfactory result with good reproducibility. This work proves that CQDs synthesized from PLFL can also be an alternative fluorescent probe for fluorescent-based sensing applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: There is no denying that nanomaterials play an essential role in shaping various areas such as biomedical and life sciences, material sciences, electronics, physical sciences, and forensic science. Nanoparticles, quantum dots (QDs), nanofibers, and carbon nanotubes are some of the examples of novel nanomaterials. The remarkable mechanical, optical, electronic, and chemical properties of these materials have made them a central topic of most scientific research up to this day [1,2]. Their relevance to today’s society has become more prominent because of the recent 2023 Nobel Prize for Chemistry, in which QDs garnered the award [3]. These QDs are fluorescent semiconductor nanomaterials that greatly influence numerous applications due to their physicochemical attributes. However, their questionable toxicity limits these QDs from pushing through further their applicability, especially in biomedical fields [4]. The continuous pursuit of new nanomaterials to defy the possible limitations of semiconductor-based QDs has led to the uncovering of a new member of QDs – they are known as “carbon quantum dots” (CQDs).

Since their accidental discovery back in 2004, the exponential growth of published scientific articles regarding carbon quantum dots is inevitable evidence of their dominance in the world of nanoscience and nanotechnology. As a new emerging class of carbon nanomaterials, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have drawn considerable attention to the scientific community due to their distinct features and excellent properties such as high biocompatibility, low cytotoxicity, high resistance to photobleaching, outstanding water solubility, inexpensive sources, and most importantly, their tunable fluorescence emission and broad emission range [5–7]. Owing to their unique properties, they have been game-changers in different applications ranging from bio- [8] and chemical sensing [9,10] (e.g., Fe3+ [11], sialic acid [12], and glutathione [13] sensing), bioimaging [14], drug delivery agents [15], photocatalysis, LED [16] and energy conversion, just to name a few. Generally, CQDs are defined as zero-dimensional and quasi-spherical carbon nanomaterials that possess a size typically less than 10 nm. They commonly consist of a carbon core, which could be amorphous or crystalline with a mixture of sp2- and sp3- hybridized carbon matrix and a rich-functional group (hydroxyl, carboxyl, carboxylate, amine, and amide) surface shell that envelops the core [6,17]. With the variation of selected precursors and synthesis techniques, the core–shell structure of CQDs and their properties can be modified to create widely diverse and multifaceted CQDs. In recent years, CQD synthesis routes have generally been divided into either bottom-up or top-down approaches. The bottom-up method produced CQDs through the dehydration and carbonization of small organic molecules (e.g., citric acid/urea [18] and L-cystine [19]) and biological materials (e.g., biowaste [20]) at high temperatures. This method includes processes such as microwaving, hydrothermal, pyrolysis, or combustion. In contrast, carbon dots in top-down synthesis are fabricated by cutting down carbonaceous materials like graphite flakes [21], charcoal powder [22], carbon fiber, and carbon nanotubes. This can be achieved through arc discharge, chemical oxidation, electrochemical methods, or laser ablation. Among all of the methods, laser ablation has increasingly gained recognition as a promising alternative to the synthesis route of CQDs because of its numerous advantages: (1) a versatile and straightforward method; (2) high production rates with high purity; (3) good control of particle size and composition by varying laser parameters; and (4) elimination of toxic by-products [23–25].

Pulsed laser ablation techniques, such as pulsed laser ablation in liquid (PLAL) and pulsed laser fragmentation in liquid (PLFL), have been implemented to fabricate different types of nanoparticles, including CQDs. PLAL is a process where CQDs are formed through the interaction between short laser pulses (
10
- 13
to
10
- 8
s) and a solid bulk target (graphite), immersed in a liquid medium (i.e., deionized water [26], acetone, DMF [27], etc.). During the laser-target interaction, most of the laser pulse energy is absorbed by the solid target, usually fixed at the bottom of the vessel. A plasma plume is then generated, which comprises an abundant population of ions, atoms, and electrons ejected from the material. The interaction between the plasma plume and the surrounding liquid formed a cavitation bubble – a thin layer of vaporized solvent. Subsequently, the cavitation bubble expands and collapses, leading to the release of nanoparticles [23,27,28]. In comparison with PLAL, the PLFL method fabricates CQDs by downsizing the starting materials (in micrometers) up to nanometer scale dispersed in a liquid medium. The underlying mechanism of the size reduction capability of PLFL is based on (1) photothermal vaporization or (2) Coulomb explosion. Photothermal vaporization occurs when the particle temperature is greater than the boiling point of the bulk material. The particles will then be ejected and trapped in a nanobubble (similar to a cavitation bubble) surrounding the bulk material. This nanobubble will facilitate the condensation of ejected atoms to produce smaller particles. In addition, the Coulomb explosion mechanism states that the ionized nanoparticles generated from the electrons ejected from the bulk will undergo spontaneous fission due to the internal charge repulsion, thereby forming nanosized particles [23,29]. Despite their exceptional mechanism of forming nanoparticles, pulsed laser techniques are still at the rear of other established routes for synthesizing CQDs. In fact, there are less than 200 scientific articles about CQDs synthesized using lasers between 2012 and 2022, based on the Scopus database [30]. This may be due to the disadvantages stapled on the pulsed laser method, such as in the PLFL method, where the major drawback is the use of organic solvents because of the hydrophobicity of the starting precursors, such as charcoal powder [31], etc. This might restrict their fullest potential to biomedical applications. Thus, it is of great importance to produce a water-stable suspension of CQDs synthesized via the PLFL method. While there has been literature dedicated to the production of water-soluble CQDs via the PLFL method, their practical application remains unexplored [31,32]. Establishing a reliable method for producing CQDs in water opens new avenues for their applicability, especially in biomedical fields.

Extensive efforts have been applied to study the maximum performance of CQDs in biomedical applications, especially in the field of sensing. One of the current interests that is still relevant up to this day, in which CQDs can be a promising candidate, is the development of a glucose sensor. Glucose is undoubtedly one of the most important carbohydrates that exist in all organisms as a source of energy for cellular activity. However, when glucose levels are abnormal and not controlled regularly, this poses serious health complications and eventually leads to diabetes. To minimize its potential risks, early diagnosis and self-intervention are critical in monitoring blood glucose levels [33]. Several glucose sensors are already available on the market; however, they usually require the aid of enzymes (i.e., glucose oxidase, glucose dehydrogenase, and hexokinase [34]), which are costly and sensitive to environmental conditions (i.e., pH, temperature, humidity) [35]. With these limitations, various non-enzymatic glucose sensors were developed and explored intensively using optical-based methods, which are either colorimetry-based or fluorescence-based. Glucose determination by using the fluorescence platform has received much attention because of its rapid response characteristics through simple processes, high sensitivity, and low detection limits. With this, CQDs can be exploited as a fluorescent material for glucose detection due to their intrinsic fluorescence property. By designing them with proper and suitable glucose-binding compounds, CQD can be an effective non-enzymatic glucose sensor. Fortunately, compounds such as boronic acid derivatives are commonly used as glucose receptors for the non-enzymatic glucose sensing approach because of their ability to bind covalently with the cis-diols of glucose [36,37]. Carbon dots functionalized with boronic acid moieties can exhibit either a fluorescence quenching (turn-off) [38] or fluorescence enhancement (turn-on) [39] response after reacting with glucose, which is the basis for evaluating their analytical performance. Owing to the low cytotoxicity of CQDs, CQDs-boronic acid structure has also been studied in vivo and in vitro for glucose monitoring [39,40]. Thus, CQD as a non-enzymatic glucose sensor can be a remarkable alternative route to the current glucose determination method.

In this study, we developed a non-enzymatic glucose sensor based on CQDs as the fluorescent probe and 3-aminophenylboronic acid (APBA) as the glucose molecule receptor. CQDs were synthesized by the pulsed laser fragmentation top-down technique, where carbon black powder (<100 nm) and ethanol were the carbon precursor and liquid medium, respectively. The as-synthesized CQDs were further re-irradiated along with oxidizing acid (HNO3) and subsequently functionalized with APBA using a simple EDC/NHS conjugation process. CQDs functionalized with APBA (CQDs-APBA) show a rapid decrease in fluorescence as glucose concentration increases (turn-off response). The sensor also exhibits a rapid response to glucose with a wide linear detection range between 0.165–8 mM and a low detection limit of approximately 165
μ
M. This method shows good sensitivity without the need for a glucose-binding enzyme. The sensor also shows minimum interference from other various substances according to their relevant concentrations in real samples. Furthermore, the applicability of this sensor to a real sample like a human saliva matrix has achieved satisfactory results and good repeatability.

Carbon nanopowder (<100 nm particle size), 3-aminophenylboronic acid hydrochloride (APBA) phosphate buffer solution (1 M PBS), 2-morpholinoethanesulfonic acid buffer (0.5 M MES), D-(+)-glucose, D-(+)-maltose monohydrate, sucrose, D-lactose monohydrate, D-(−)-fructose, ascorbic acid (AA), urea, L-glutathione, L-cysteine, L-phenylalanine, sodium chloride (NaCl), Sephadex® G-25 Fine, and N-Hydroxysulfosuccinimide sodium salt (NHS) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Co. (USA). Ethanol absolute (Pharmpur®) and hydrochloric acid solution (HCl, 1 mol/L) were purchased from Scharlab, S.L., Spain. Sodium hydroxide (NaOH) pellets were purchased from Honeywell, Inc. 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC) was purchased from Thermo Fisher Scientific. Potassium chloride (KCl) and nitric acid 65 % (HNO3) were purchased from J.T. Baker, Fisher Scientific. Milli-QTM water (resistivity of 18.2 M
Ω
∙ cm, 25°C) from the Milli-Q system by Wasserlab (Ultramatic) was used throughout the experiments. All of the chemical reagents were used without any further purification.

The optical properties of the samples were investigated by their absorbance and fluorescence properties. The absorbance spectra were recorded using a Cary (Varian) 500 Scan UV–VIS-NIR spectrophotometer, while the photoluminescence (PL) characterizations were carried out using a JASCO J-1500 Circular Dichroism (CD) Spectrometer equipped with a 150 W Xenon (Xe) lamp and circulating thermostat unit. The time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) was measured through a PL spectrophotometer (Fluorolog 3–11, Horiba) at 405 nm pulsed laser (1 MHz frequency, NanoLED-405L, <100 ps of pulse width). The samples for optical characterizations were in liquid form and were analyzed in quartz cuvettes with a 10 mm path length. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) was used to examine the morphological information of the samples and was carried out using JEOL JEM 2100 equipment operating at 200 kV of accelerating voltage. The sample for HR-TEM images was prepared by depositing drops of the colloidal solution on a 200-mesh carbon-coated copper TEM grid and allowing it to dry under a vacuum oven. Raman spectra were measured using a JASCO NRS-5100 Laser Raman Spectrometer. 0.1 mL of colloidal solution was dropped onto a glass substrate and dried in a vacuum oven at 50 °C, then a Raman spectrum of resolution 1cm−1 was acquired using a UV laser line of wavelength 325.03 nm operating at a laser power of 1.1 mW. The elemental and chemical composition of the samples were investigated using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The measured spectra were collected by utilizing the K-ALPHA Thermo Scientific spectrometer system. All spectra were analyzed with monochromatized Al K
α
radiation (E=1486.86 eV) having a spot size of 250
μ
m working at 30 mA and 2 kV. The analysis was conducted by obtaining the survey spectra with a pass energy of 200 eV and high-resolution spectra of particular elements using 50 eV pass energy. Prior to measurement, the colloidal suspension was deposited evenly on a TEM grid and dried under a 50°C vacuum oven. The chemical state of the samples was further studied by using a Fourier Transform Infrared spectrometer (FTIR-6200 JASCO). FTIR spectra were collected using the attenuated total reflectance (ATR) technique over the range of 4000cm−1 – 400cm−1 with a resolution of 4cm−1. Aliquots of the solution were dried on aluminum foil and pressed in contact with a diamond crystal to obtain the spectra. Zeta potential (
ζ
) measurements were carried out by using a Zetasizer Nano-ZS90 (Malvern Instruments, UK).

The quantum yield (QY) of the prepared samples was determined by using the relative method, where quinine sulfate (QY = 0.54 in 0.1 M H2SO4) was used as a reference fluorophore [27,41]. The QY was calculated using the following equation (1):
(1)
QY
s
=
QY
R
×
A R
A s
×
I s
I R
×
n s
n R
2
where A is the optical density, I is the integrated fluorescence emission, n is the refractive index, and the subscripts “S” and “R” represent the sample and reference fluorophore, respectively. The absorbance at the excitation wavelength was kept below 0.1 to avoid inner filter effects and obtain more reliable results.

CQDs were synthesized using a facile, simple, and straightforward PLFL technique from carbon black nanopowder suspended in high-purity ethanol as a liquid medium. Initially, 12 mg of carbon powder was dispersed in 6 mL of ethanol in a glass vial. The resulting black solution was then irradiated with a horizontal unfocused laser pulse (532 nm, 2
ω
harmonic) from a Q-switch Nd:YAG (Neodymium-Doped Yttrium Aluminum Garnet) laser system (VIBRANT, Model 355 II) at a repetition rate of 10 Hz and a pulse duration of 10 ns. The laser fragmentation was operated at room temperature for 3 h at a pulse energy of 130 mJ and a fluence of approximately 0.26 J/cm2 (beam diameter
≈
8 mm) at the surface of the glass vial. During the irradiation, magnetic stirring was utilized to ensure total fragmentation and to avoid the gravitational settling of carbon powders. After the fragmentation process, a dark yellow colloidal solution was obtained, indicating the formation of fluorescent CQDs. Scheme 1 shows the schematic setup to produce CQDs via laser fragmentation (PLFL).

With the same laser parameters as indicated above, 3 mL of the laser-synthesized CQDs was mixed further with 2 mL of water (1 mL of water was added every 15 min) while under re-irradiation. Afterwards, 0.5 mL of HNO3 was added to the yellowish solution and re-irradiated again with a laser for 2 h. The product was then filtered through a 0.2
μ
m microporous filter to remove some large products. This process oxidized the surface of CQDs to increase their water dispersibility for easy functionalization in this study [40,42]. This also offers an alternative method to produce water-soluble CQDs, which is difficult to achieve via PLFL owing to the hydrophobicity of the starting precursors. Note that, without the re-irradiation process, the direct addition of water resulted in the aggregation of CQDs. Fig. S1 shows the comparison of CQDs and acid-treated CQDs dispersed in water and in PBS solution. These acid-treated CQDs will be referred to as the “oxidized-CQDs” throughout this study.

After the acid treatment, 1 mL of the oxidized-CQDs was mixed with 5 mL of 0.1 M PBS (pH = 7.4) to attain a volume ratio of 1:5. Then, CQDs functionalized with APBA (CQDs-APBA) were obtained using the EDC/NHS coupling mechanism as previously reported [43]. 13 mg of EDC and 7 mg of NHS were mixed into the final solution with PBS and stirred in a dark room overnight at room temperature. After, 11 mg of APBA was mixed with the solution and stirred in the dark for 1 day. The final product was filtered through a 0.22
μ
m microporous filter and purified further by using size exclusion chromatography (SEC) to remove unreacted entities. Scheme 1 presents the functionalization process to produce CQDs-APBA.

Size-exclusion chromatography (SEC) was carried out by using Sephadex® G-25 Fine resin (Particle size = 20 – 80
μ
m) and disposable columns from CytivaTM (inner diameter = 15 mm; height = 13 cm). First, 5g of the dry resin was swollen overnight using freshly prepared Milli-Q water at room temperature for 1 day. The same Milli-Q water was also used as the mobile phase [44]. Before packing the column, air bubbles were removed by shaking the gel solution manually and gently. Then, the slurry solution was slowly poured into the column. After the excess water was eluted and the beads settled in the column, the remaining gel suspension was added gradually to reach the desired height of the stationary phase (approximately 6 cm). Throughout the packing process, the column was never allowed to run dry; that is, the solvent level was mostly above the stationary phase. The column was further washed with 10 mL of water. Then, 1.5 mL of CQDs-APBA solution was dropped carefully so as not to disturb the bed column and distributed evenly on the bed surface, forming a thin band. When all the sample solution was completely inside the stationary phase, deionized water was slowly added to the column. The fractions were collected at the bottom of the gel column using the 1.5 mL microtubes. Then, the purified CQDs-APBA were obtained from the fractions that display intense fluorescence under an ultraviolet (UV) lamp and strong fluorescence emission measured by a spectrometer. Furthermore, the absorbance of purified CQDs-APBA was measured and compared to the later eluted fractions (higher fractions). Fig. S2 shows a typical SEC set-up described in this study and the corresponding collected fractions under a UV lamp with the measured fluorescence spectra of the selected fractions. Fig. S3(a) shows the absorbance spectra of the purified CQDs-APBA and the selected eluted fractions. The absorbance spectra of the solution containing free EDC, free NHS, and free APBA were also measured for comparison as shown in Fig. S3(b). The absorbance spectra of higher fractions were mostly from free and unreacted EDC, NHS, and APBA molecules as previously known in Scheme 1.

The sensing solution was prepared by pipetting 60
μ
l of the purified CQDs-APBA into a series of 5 mL microtubes. Then, 3 mL of 0.1 M PBS (pH = 10.4) was mixed with the tubes containing CQDs-APBA. Different amounts of glucose were later added. After that, the final mixtures were diluted to a final volume of 3.5 mL with deionized water and mixed thoroughly using a vortex stirrer (RS-LAB 6PRO) for 5 min due to its rapid response. The fluorescence of the samples was then measured with a spectrometer at room temperature under an excitation wavelength of 240 nm. The detection of glucose towards real analytes such as saliva was also performed in this study. The glucose level of the treated human saliva samples was examined under the same experimental conditions as mentioned above. The saliva was procured from a volunteer. Section S1 summarized the saliva collection and treatment protocol employed in this study, which was followed by previous researchers with a few adjustments [45,46].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural and surface characterization of the prepared samples
ce:section.ce:para:
The morphology and size of the synthesized samples, namely CQDs (as-prepared) and CQDs-APBA, were investigated by using Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM). Fig. 1 a shows a low-resolution TEM image of one of the smaller pieces (Fig. 1a inset) that confined some of the CQDs after the laser fragmentation process. This shows the transformation of a bulk carbon powder (Fig. S4) as it interacts with the nanosecond laser pulses [47]. The as-prepared CQDs in ethanol show a minimum aggregation after the drying process, which was also observed in previously reported literature [27,31]. However, some of the CQDs are visible and isolated with a quasi-spherical geometry having a broad size distribution ranging from 2.5 to 10.5 nm with a mean size of 5.1
±
2.2 nm, as shown in the histogram (inset of Fig. 1b), which was fitted by a Gaussian distribution curve. In Fig. 1b, a high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) image of CQDs shows the presence of lattice fringes with a spacing of 0.20, 0.21, and 0.23 nm, which corresponds to the (100) plane of graphitic type carbon structure [27,46]. The variety of interplanar spacing that exists within CQDs suggests that laser fragmentation of carbon black powder in ethanol can produce more than one crystalline structure, as previously shown [27]. After treating the CQDs with a strong oxidizer such as nitric acid (oxidized-CQDs), Fig. S5(a) displays carbon nanoparticles that are now well-dispersed and almost uniform in size without agglomeration. Fig. S5(b) shows that the size distribution of oxidized-CQDs becomes narrower with a slight deviation in mean size (5.2
±
1.6 nm). This suggests that the strong acid, along with laser re-irradiation, exfoliates some of the large CQDs, thereby reducing their size. Fig. 1c shows a TEM image of CQDs-APBA after SEC purification. CQDs-APBA is also monodispersed, with a narrower size distribution than CQDs, ranging from 1.2 nm to 4.2 nm, with an average size of 1.8
±
0.6 nm (inset of Fig. 1d). The average size decreases compared to CQDs and oxidized-CQDs due to the separation and removal of relatively large nanoparticles during SEC treatment [44]. In Fig. 1d, a magnified image of CQDs-APBA indicates the presence of a lattice with a spacing of 0.31 nm, which can be attributed to the (002) plane of the graphitic framework [38]. This particular spacing was also found in CQDs, as shown in Fig. S6. In addition, both CQDs and CQDs-APBA display both crystalline graphitic structure and some amorphous phases, as indicated by their HRTEM images, which can be expected for the laser synthesis technique of carbon nanomaterials [27,31,47–49].

Raman spectroscopy was also employed to further characterize the structural properties of the prepared samples. Fig. 2 shows the Raman spectra of the samples, which have two characteristic peaks that are typically found in most carbonaceous materials, like carbon dots. The peaks located at 1363 cm−1, 1375 cm−1, and 1378 cm−1 are associated with the D-bands of CQDs, oxidized-CQDs, and CQDs-APBA, respectively [48,50]. This D-band is due to the presence of structural defects and disorder within the graphitic carbon and is related to the sp3 hybrid orbital of carbon atoms [26,48]. Meanwhile, the peaks positioned at 1604 cm−1, 1605 cm−1, and 1607 cm−1 are associated with the G-bands of CQDs, oxidized-CQDs, and CQDs-APBA, respectively. This G-band arises due to the in-plane vibration of sp2-hybridized carbon [27,48]. The degree of disorder can be quantified by calculating the ratio of the intensities of the D and G-bands (ID/IG). The ratio ID/IG of CQDs was found to be 0.20 and increased to 0.29 after oxidation (oxidized-CQDs) and continued to rise to 0.35 after functionalization with APBA (CQDs-APBA). This suggests that the as-prepared CQDs possess a higher degree of crystallinity, which is in good agreement with their HR-TEM image in comparison to CQDs-APBA [27,51]. Moreover, the gradual increase in ratio after chemical oxidation suggested that the oxygen-containing functional groups formed on the carbon dots’ surface resulted in a more disordered structure [27,52]. After functionalization with APBA, the ratio increases further, which means that the breaking of bonds and altering the surface functionality of CQDs reinforced some sp3 carbon domains [53].

To identify different functional groups that exist on the surface of the synthesized samples, FTIR spectra were measured as shown in Fig. 3 . A broad peak centered approximately at 3451 cm−1 on CQDs is associated with (O–H) stretching vibration. The peaks at 2971 cm−1 and 2925 cm−1 are assigned to sp2 and sp3(C–H) stretching, respectively. The (C=O) stretching vibration was observed at 1714 cm−1 and the peak at 1617 cm−1 is attributed to the (C=C) stretching of aromatic carbon. The peaks located at 1438 cm−1 and 1382 cm−1 are both associated with (C–H) bending vibration. The other visible peaks positioned at 1238 cm−1, 1090 cm−1, and 1064 cm−1 are assigned to (C-OH) stretching, (C-O-C) asymmetric stretching, and (C-O) stretching vibrations, respectively [54]. The formation of functional groups on the surface of laser-produced CQDs is due to the photothermal decomposition of the solvent molecules (ethanol, in this case) during the laser fragmentation process [27,31,47]. Furthermore, after chemical oxidation with HNO3, the (O–H) stretching vibration centered at 3272 cm−1 becomes broader and stronger in comparison to the as-prepared CQDs. This further confirmed that acid treatment enhances the aqueous dispersibility of CQDs, as already shown in Fig. S1. Moreover, the new absorption peaks of (C=O) stretching and (C=C) stretching of aromatic carbon were observed at 1694 cm−1 and 1640 cm−1, respectively. The formation of peaks at 1563 cm−1 and 1343 cm−1 is attributed to (N–H) bending and (C-N) stretching vibrations, respectively, which confirmed the presence of (–NO2) groups attached to the surface of carbon dots due to acid treatment. The presence of other peaks at 2980 cm−1, 1406 cm−1, 1099 cm−1, and 1048 cm−1 are assigned to (C–H) stretching, (C–H) bending, (C-O-C) asymmetric stretching, and (C-O) stretching vibrations, respectively [49,52]. In addition, a strong broad peak centered at approximately 3224 cm−1 was also observed in the CQDs-APBA spectra which is assigned to (O–H) stretching vibration owing to the (B-OH) moiety of APBA. The notable peak shift of (C=O) from 1694 cm−1 (as observed in oxidized-CQDs) to 1632 cm−1 proves the secondary amide covalent bond formation between APBA and CQDs. The shoulder peak at 3355 cm−1 can be assigned to (N–H) stretching vibrations of a secondary amide, which further verifies the presence of an amide. The appearance of peaks at 1333 cm−1, 1094 cm−1, and 1038 cm−1 are attributed to (B-O) stretching, (C-B) stretching, and (B-OH) deformation vibrations, respectively [20,55,56]. These peaks indicated the successful incorporation of APBA onto the surface of carbon dots.

In addition to FTIR, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was also carried out to provide in-depth information about the elemental composition and the chemical state of the samples. The XPS survey spectra of CQDs (Fig. 4 a) revealed that they are composed of 84.72% carbon (C 1s) and 15.29% oxygen (O 1s). On the other hand, oxidized-CQDs consist of 54.6% carbon (C 1s), 39.84% oxygen (O 1s), and 5.55% nitrogen (N 1s), as displayed in their survey spectra (Fig. S7(a)). The increase in oxygen content in this sample is a consequence of oxidizing CQDs with strong acid. The high-resolution C 1s spectra of CQDs in Fig. 4c can be deconvoluted into four peaks centered approximately at ∼ 284.5, ∼285.4, ∼286.4, and ∼ 288.1 eV, which were assigned to C–C or C=C (sp2 carbon), C-OH (hydroxyl), C=O (carbonyl), and O-C=O (carboxyl), respectively [12,55–57]. Out of the four peaks, the sp2 carbon is the most intense peak, which indicates a graphitic structure after laser fragmentation, as observed before in their Raman spectra. Table S1 further shows that sp2 carbon quantitatively obtained 49.2% atomic percent (atomic %) which is the highest percentage among the four peaks. In addition, the O 1s spectra of CQDs can be resolved into three peaks positioned at ∼ 531.4, ∼532.6, and ∼ 534.1 eV which correspond to O=C, O-C, and O=C-O, respectively [31,38,58]. Fig. S7(b) shows the deconvoluted C 1s spectra of the oxidized sample, which display identical peaks to CQDs with the addition of C-N, whereas the O 1s spectra exhibit only two peaks, which are attributed to O-C=O (∼532.1 eV) and O-C (∼532.8 eV), which were also found in CQDs (Fig. S7(c)). Moreover, there is a significant increase in the atomic % of O-C=O (carboxylic groups) from 0.41% in CQDs to 22.53% in oxidized-CQDs which is more suitable in this study for easy functionalization. Subsequently, the C=C/C–C content decreases from 49.2% in CQDs to 34.68% in oxidized-CQDs. This implies that some of the C–C and C=C bonds break and are attached to oxygen-rich functional groups during the oxidation and laser irradiation processes [59]. The N 1s spectra of oxidized-CQDs were also measured and can be divided into two peaks located at ∼ 399.6 and ∼ 406.9 eV, which were assigned to C-N-C (N in pyridinic form) and –NO2 (nitro), respectively, as shown in Fig. S7(d). After functionalization with APBA, the full XPS survey spectra of CQDs-APBA as shown in Fig. 4b revealed that they are mainly comprised of four elements: 85.93 % carbon (C1s), 11.87% oxygen (O 1s), 1.52% nitrogen (N 1s), and 0.69% boron (B 1s). The increase in carbon composition relative to the oxidized sample is because APBA has an abundant C=C in its aromatic ring. The C 1s spectra of CQDs-APBA can be divided into four peaks positioned approximately at ∼ 283.6, ∼284.6, ∼285.9, and ∼ 288.1 eV, which were assigned to C-B, C–C or C=C (sp2 carbon), C-N or C-O (hydroxyl), and N-C=O or C=O (carboxyl), respectively (Fig. 4e). Additionally, the binding energies at ∼ 531.2, ∼532.6, and ∼ 533.8 eV from the deconvoluted high-resolution O 1s spectra were attributed to N-C=O, C-OH, and B-O, respectively (Fig. 4f). The N 1s spectra exhibited three distinct peaks centered at ∼ 399.5, ∼400.8, and ∼ 402.1 eV, corresponding to C-N-C (pyridinic N), N–H (pyrrolic N), and (C)3-N (graphitic N), respectively (Fig. 4g). In Fig. 4h, the B 1s spectra exhibited only two distinct peaks at ∼ 188.7 and ∼ 191.2 eV, corresponding to B-C and B-OH, respectively [12,20,38,55,56,58]. The presence of N-C=O in C 1s and O 1s spectra confirms the formation of amide during EDC/NHS coupling between the oxidized sample and APBA. Furthermore, the known peaks C-B, B-OH, and B-O from the spectra show that the APBA is well incorporated into the CQD surface. The XPS spectra of all these samples corroborate well with their FTIR and Raman results. The XPS, FTIR.

and Raman spectra of CQDs-APBA also coincide well with their proposed structure, as shown in Scheme 1. All atomic % of functional groups deconvoluted from the XPS spectra of the samples were summarized in Tables S1–S3.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of CQDs and CQDs-APBA
ce:section.ce:para:
The optical characterizations of CQDs and CQDs-APBA were explored using UV–Vis absorbance and fluorescence spectroscopy. The UV–Vis of CQDs, as shown in Fig. 5 a, displays various absorption peaks, which is a typical curve of carbon nanoparticles synthesized with laser in ethanol [31,49,51]. The peak near 225 nm is a common absorption of sp-hybridized carbon structure polyynes (C8H2), which are the usual by-products of laser synthesis [31,48]. The as-prepared CQDs also exhibit an absorption band between 230 and 280 nm with a strong peak at ∼ 252 nm associated with the
π →
π
∗
transition of sp2 hybridized aromatic structure of their carbon core (C=C). The absorption ranges from 300 to 330 nm with a peak centered approximately at 325 nm, representing the
n →
π
∗
transition of C=O bonds on the surface of CQDs. Moreover, CQDs-APBA exhibits a broad absorption that varies from the as-prepared CQDs. This is due to the treatment and functionalization effects of APBA on CQDs. The inset of Fig. 5a is a magnified absorbance curve of CQDs-APBA between 250 and 450 nm which displays a weak absorbance band centered at ∼ 310 nm from the
n →
π
∗
transition of C=O bonds. To compare with CQDs-APBA, the inset also includes the absorbance curve of the oxidized-CQDs centered at ∼ 300 nm with a shoulder peak at ∼ 280 nm, which confirmed the absorbance shift after functionalization. In addition, the APBA aqueous solution exhibits two absorbance peaks at ∼ 234 nm and ∼ 296 nm (Fig. S8) that deviate from CQDs-APBA absorbance, suggesting that APBA is well-incorporated into CQDs. Furthermore, the absorbance spectra of CQDs and CQDs-APBA show that their absorption tails extend up to the visible region, suggesting the presence of nanocarbons as previously found in other literature [48,56].

One of the interesting properties of CQDs that gain attention among scientific groups is their fluorescence, on which most of their practical applications are based. Fig. 5b shows the photoluminescence (PL) and PL excitation (PLE) spectra of synthesized CQDs with maximum emission at 450 nm excited at its excitation maximum of 380 nm. As shown, the synthesized CQDs dispersed in ethanol exhibit strong blue fluorescence under a UV lamp (inset of Fig. 5b). Similar to most reported CQDs, these CQDs also exhibit excitation wavelength-dependent spectra, as shown in Fig. 5c. That is, the PL peaks red-shift as the excitation wavelength increases with varying intensities. As observed in the inset of Fig. 5c, it shows how the luminescence of CQDs changes color at different laser wavelengths. The reason for this fascinating behavior is still up for debate among researchers. However, this kind of PL profile of CQDs can be explained by these two mechanisms: (1) the quantum confinement effect (known as the “size effect”) and (2) emissive surface traps, which are related to functional groups. The former states that the excitation-dependent behavior of CQDs may be due to the wide distribution of size. The smaller the size, the wider the band gap, and the shorter the emission wavelength. The latter mechanism states that the functional groups (–COOH, –OH, and C-O-C) can generate different surface energy states, which provides an alternative relaxation pathway for the electron-hole pair (exciton) [31,48,60]. A broad PL emission of CQDs was also observed in Fig. 5c, which can be linked to the total contribution of radiative recombination of excitons from CQDs’ carbogenic core (
π →
π
∗
transition of C=C) and surface functional groups (
n →
π
∗
transition) [27].

The PL emission intensity of the oxidized-CQDs decreases abruptly, as shown in Fig. 5d, which is caused by the increase of electron-withdrawing functional groups on their surface, like –NO2 and O-C=O/–COOH, as shown by their XPS results. These functional groups that were attached to the edges of the sp2-conjugated
π
-domains of the oxidized-CQDs are usually the nonradiative recombination centers of excitons, thereby reducing their emission intensities [52,61]. In addition, the sudden decrease in emission intensities of oxidized-CQDs can also be attributed to the decrease in pH after the addition of nitric acid as previously reported [62]. After attaching APBA to the surface of oxidized-CQDs, the CQDs-APBA emission intensity suddenly increased and blue-shifted to an emission peak of ∼ 376 nm. The reaction through EDC/NHS formed an amide, which diminished the population of nonradiative recombination centers (–COOH) on the CQD surface. The amides on CQDs are known to be a radiative route for excitons and, thus, will greatly increase the fluorescence intensity of CQDs. In addition, the blue-shift effect of the emission peak after functionalization can be linked to the conjugation effect of APBA and CQDs [20,38]. The fluorescence of CQDs-APBA shows a deep violet emission under a 254 nm lamp and a blue emission under 365 nm, as shown in the inset of Fig. 5d. On the other hand, CQDs-APBA exhibited an excitation-independent wavelength PL profile (Fig. 5e), which may have been caused by (1) their narrow size distribution or (2) the stable CQDs-APBA structure where APBA units act as the single emitter [55,60,63]. The PLE spectra of CQDs-APBA displayed two peaks: ∼240 nm and ∼ 300 nm, as shown in Fig. S9(a). Furthermore, CQDs-APBA exhibits two weak emission peaks in the visible region: a narrow peak at ∼ 466 nm and a broad peak centered at ∼ 550 nm after exciting at 405 nm, as shown in Fig. S9(b), which may be from the surface state dominance at the longer wavelength [64].

The quantum yield (QY) of CQDs and CQDs-APBA were measured using the relative method, where quinine sulfate (QY = 54% in 0.1 M H2SO4) was used as a reference [41]. The excitation wavelengths 360 nm and 300 nm were used in the QY calculations of CQDs and CQDs-APBA, respectively. The QY of CQDs in ethanol was found to be 0.42% (Fig. S10(a)), which is expected for the laser synthesis technique of carbon dots [14,27,48]. Moreover, the QY of CQDs-APBA in PBS solution increased to 35.4% (Fig. S10(b)) (∼80-fold higher than the as-prepared CQDs) as expected after functionalization [20,38]. Table S4 and S5 summarize the information from the QY graph of CQDs and CQDs-APBA, respectively. The PL decay curves of CQDs and CQDs-APBA, as shown in Fig. 6 a and 6b, respectively, were determined by using a time-correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) technique (excitation at 405 nm) and were fitted by a two-term exponential function. The two lifetimes from the decay curves can be interpreted as the surface state, which is known to have a longer recombination lifetime, and the intrinsic state (carbon core), which has a relatively short lifetime [65]. The lifetimes of CQDs are 1.09 ns and 5.08 ns with an average lifetime of 4.14 ns. The short lifetime has a 59% contribution, which is quite higher than the longer lifetime (41%), suggesting a relatively higher amount of carbogenic core. The lifetimes of CQDs-APBA are 1.78 ns (30%) and 6.88 ns (70%), with an average lifetime of 6.38 ns. The longer lifetime has a higher contribution which suggests an increased number of surface states due to modification and functionalization. The exponential fitting model and the average lifetime calculation were explicitly elucidated in Section S2.

Boronic acid derivatives have been extensively used in saccharide detection due to their ability to bind and form stable boronate esters with cis-diols from carbohydrates such as glucose. To produce an effective glucose sensor with the aid of a boronic acid moiety as a receptor for glucose, a fluorescent probe such as CQDs is required to report the binding event, which, in consequence, can increase (turn-on response) or decrease (turn-off response) the fluorescence of CQDs upon binding with glucose molecules. Thus, the current engineered aminophenylboronic acid-functionalized CQDs (CQDs-APBA) can be a promising candidate as a fluorescent glucose sensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect and optimization of pH condition
ce:section.ce:para:
The effect of the pH environment on the fluorescence emission of CQDs-APBA was also investigated. The pH environment of CQDs-APBA was changed by using 100 mM MES and 100 mM PBS buffer titrated with 1 M HCl and 1 M NaOH to reach the desired pH value. Fig. 7 a shows that CQDs-APBA fluorescence emission reaches its maximum when pH = 7.5. Additionally, when pH < 7.5, their fluorescence intensity starts to increase, and when pH > 7.5, the intensity begins to drop. The change in fluorescence intensity with the pH variation may be due to the deprotonation and protonation of the extra carboxylic groups, as observed in most CQDs. Another underlying reason may also be the APBA molecules attached to the surface of carbon dots, in which the anionic form is less fluorescent than the neutral form [36,54,66]. Furthermore, the optimization of glucose sensing was solely based on varying the pH environment of CQDs-APBA. 3-aminophenylboronic acid (APBA) molecules, like any other boronic acid-containing compound, are Lewis acid and can form a cyclic ester with the 1, 2 or 1, 3-diols of glucose molecules due to the vacant p-orbital in their boron atom. The boron atom in boronic acid exists in sp2-hybridization trigonal planar as its neutral form, while at higher pH, boron takes the form of tetrahedral sp3-hybridization. Although both forms can react with glucose, the anionic tetrahedral form of boronic acid has a higher binding affinity compared to its neutral form [36,37]. This means that the interaction of boronic acid with glucose diols can be maximized by considering the pH. Thus, to optimize the glucose sensing ability of the designed sensor, this study investigated the effect of pH on the fluorescent signal of CQDs-APBA immediately after binding with glucose. Fig. 7b shows the response of CQDs-APBA at different pH upon binding with 1 mM of glucose. In this study, the response is evaluated by calculating the relative intensity change (F0 /F), where F and F0 are the fluorescent intensities of CQDs-APBA with and without glucose, respectively: F0 /F < 1 (turn-on response), F0 /F = 1 (no response), and F0 /F > 1 (turn-off response). The pH environment of CQDs-APBA was changed by using the same method as Fig. 7a. At acidic pH < 7 and pH = 7, there are no significant changes in the fluorescence of CQDs-APBA. This is because, at this pH region, the neutral trigonal form of boronic acid is dominant, which does not bind strongly with glucose due to the pKa (dissociation constant) of APBA reportedly being 8.8 [36]. When pH = 9 (pH > pKa), the anionic form dominates, and a significant turn-off response was observed. The maximum decrease in fluorescence was attained when pH approached 10.4. At a more alkaline pH > 10.4, the fluorescence change decreases due to the higher concentration of
O H
-
ions that deprotonate the surface of CQDs and induce electrostatic repulsion among CQDs-APBA, and thus, the CQDs-APBA-glucose complex will not be more favored. Thus, a pH of 10.4 was used throughout the sensing experiments. The fluorescence emission at the optimal sensing pH is not as great as when pH is 7.5; however, the relative intensity change (F0 /F) is the crucial value for the part of sensing.

Under the optimized conditions explored above, the CQDs-APBA fluorescence response to different concentrations of glucose was investigated. Fig. 8 a shows that as the glucose concentration increases, the fluorescence intensity of CQDs-APBA decreases (turn-off response). This response was evaluated by measuring the relative intensity change (F0 /F) as utilized above. A Stern-Volmer plot was drawn between the ratio F0 /F and glucose concentrations, as shown in Fig. 8b. The sensor can respond for as low as 0.1 mM and exhibits a good wide linear range between 0.165 mM to 8 mM, with a good correlation coefficient of
R
2
=
0.9921. The limit of detection (LOD) was measured to be 165
μ
M which is comparable to or better than those of previous boronic-acid-based sensors (Table S6). The effective PL quenching of CQDs-APBA with an increase in glucose concentration is due to the formation of an anionic boronic acid–glucose ester (glucoboronate ester) between the boronic acid and the diols of glucose, as shown in Fig. 8c. Moreover, the cyclic esters are more Lewis acidic than the boronic acid, which changes their electronic properties, leading to the quenching of fluorescence. Furthermore, the CQDs-APBA-glucose complex formation formed an aggregation as a consequence of the existence of two cis-pair diols of glucose structure, which further facilitates effective fluorescence quenching [36,38,66]. The formation of the non-fluorescent complex between glucose and CQDs-APBA can be ascribed to static quenching due to the indiscernible change of fluorescence lifetime with the addition of glucose having an average fluorescence lifetime of ∼ 2.22 ns as shown in Fig. S11. This quenching mechanism was also observed in other boronic acid-based sensors [12,40,55]. The relative decrease in fluorescence lifetime of CQDs-APBA (Fig. 6b) was due to the change of pH environment in which, at this time pH = 10.4 was used instead of water [62]. Fig. 8d shows a TEM image of an obvious aggregation after adding 40 mM of glucose to the CQDs-APBA solution, which proves the complexation between glucose and CQDs-APBA. In addition to this, the zeta potential (
ζ
) of CQDs-APBA with and without glucose was also investigated to further confirm this aggregation. Figs. S12 and S13 show the zeta potential distribution of CQDs-APBA without and with glucose, respectively. The zeta potential of CQDs-APBA changes from
ζ = - 26
mV to
ζ = - 19
mV after the incorporation of glucose, which suggests the formation of CQDs-APBA-glucose assembly. On the other hand, the fluorescence intensity of the fabricated CQDs-APBA decreased inappreciably even after two months of storage, as shown in Fig. 8e. This indicates that the CQDs-APBA possess high resistance to photobleaching and excellent fluorescence stability, which are also advantageous for fluorescent-based sensors.

Apart from sensitivity, selectivity is also a critical parameter in evaluating the sensing performance of the current sensor. The effect on the sensing system of potential interfering substances such as amino acids, common ions, and other saccharides that can coexist in real samples was also investigated. The selectivity test was carried out the same way as the glucose detection procedure by replacing glucose with different possible interfering species of a specific concentration. Fig. 8f (i) shows that common ions found in body fluids, such as
K
+
(20 mM),
C l
-
(20 mM) and
N a
+
(10 mM) ions have a minimum quenching effect in comparison to 1 mM of glucose. It also shows that various compounds such as ascorbic acid (10
μ
M), urea (300
μ
M), glutathione (10
μ
M), cysteine (10
μ
M), and phenylalanine (10
μ
M) with a concentration based on a 20-fold dilution of those in serum have a little quenching effect in comparison to glucose [56]. The interference of common ions was limited due to the lack of recognition sites on CQDs-APBA. The interference study was also extended to different saccharides that have a structure analogous to glucose, such as fructose, maltose, lactose, and sucrose. Disaccharides like maltose, lactose, and sucrose (all with 0.5 mM) have a less quenching effect, as shown in Fig. 8f (ii), despite having more cis-diol units compared to glucose. Even at the same concentration of 5 mM, the quenching effect is not that high compared to 5 mM glucose (Fig. S14). This suggests that the CQDs-APBA have good selectivity among disaccharides. However, monosaccharides like fructose have a significant quenching of the fluorescence of CQDs-APBA compared to glucose at the same concentration of 5 mM (Fig. S14 inset). The possible reason behind this is that the binding affinity of boronic acid to fructose is higher than that of glucose, as reported in some literature [36,55]. Although the sensor does not offer good selectivity towards fructose, the interference of this compound can be neglected in practical applications since the blood glucose level (healthy individual: 3.9 – 6.9 mM; diabetic: 7.2 – 10 mM) is much higher compared to fructose [67–69]. For this study, the interference of fructose kept at a concentration of 0.01 mM is little compared to 1 mM of glucose, as shown in Fig. 8f (ii). Thus, the present method offers good sensitivity and selectivity for glucose, which can be suitable for glucose determination methods in real-world applications.

To assess the practical application of the current method, the sensing of glucose towards real analytes such as human saliva was also investigated. Standard addition and recovery methods were used to evaluate the performance of the sensor in human saliva. The saliva was spiked with different known concentrations of glucose: 1 mM, 3 mM, and 5 mM. Table S7 shows a good recovery of spiked glucose from 98% − 107% which suggests the good reproducibility of the current method. This means that the sensor can detect glucose in a complex matrix, such as human saliva.

Fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were successfully synthesized by pulsed laser fragmentation (PLFL) through an Nd:YAG nanosecond laser. To attain more compatible CQDs with bio applications, we further developed a method to produce a stable suspension of CQDs in water. A modification through a re-irradiation process with the aid of nitric acid was added. This method provides high-purity CQDs, which reduces the number of purification steps needed. These CQDs were also successfully utilized as fluorescent probes for glucose detection. A non-enzymatic glucose approach was achieved by using 3-aminophenylboronic acid (APBA) as the glucose molecule receptor. The covalent conjugation of this compound to the surface of CQDs via the EDC/NHS process increases the fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of CQDs to 35%. Functionalization by APBA tuned the fluorescence property of CQDs, resulting in a blue-shifted and excitation wavelength-independent emission. CQDs functionalized with APBA (CQDs-APBA) exhibited fluorescence quenching (turn-off response) in the presence of glucose. They showed good sensitivity to glucose, with a wide linear detection range between 0.165 mM to 8 mM and a limit of detection of 165
μ
M. The sensor possessed a high resistance to photobleaching and good selectivity among interfering compounds. The sensor also achieved a satisfactory evaluation of its performance in real analytes such as saliva via the recovery method. These results showed that CQDs-APBA can be an excellent alternative for turn-off fluorescent-based glucose determination in future clinical diagnostics. Thus, fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) synthesized from a nonconventional laser fragmentation method can also be an excellent candidate as fluorescent probes in fluorescent-based sensing applications.
